text: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments
textPreview: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments

alt: AI Playbook for Public Health logo

text:
Welcome participants to the AI-Supported Public Health Workflows lesson.
Explain that this deck is designed for staff training, leadership orientation, governance preparation, or cross-program discussion.
Frame the topic in plain language: AI can support signal detection, evidence gathering, analysis, synthesis, briefing, communication, and decision support.
Invite participants to think about where this topic already appears in their department's workflows, vendor tools, data systems, communications, or planning conversations.
AI can support signal detection, evidence gathering, analysis, synthesis, briefing, communication, and decision support.
textPreview: AI can support signal detection, evidence gathering, analysis, synthesis, briefing, communication, and decision support.

text: AI projects often fail when they are not anchored in a real workflow. Public health work includes handoffs, legal authorities, partner relationships, community trust, and time-sensitive decisions. Workflow mapping helps agencies find where AI.
textPreview: AI projects often fail when they are not anchored in a real workflow. Public health work includes handoffs, legal authorities, partner re...

text: Match the tool to workflow stage and keep humans responsible for decisions.
textPreview: Match the tool to workflow stage and keep humans responsible for decisions.

text:
Use this slide in the AI-Supported Public Health Workflows training module.
Use this slide to explain the practical public health reason for learning this topic before AI tools are approved or deployed.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 7, 10, 11 and Tools 31, 29, 41.
Emphasize the core guardrail: Match the tool to workflow stage and keep humans responsible for decisions.
Ask participants how this topic could affect their own program area, staff roles, community relationships, data, or public communications.
By the end of this lesson, staff should be able to:
textPreview: By the end of this lesson, staff should be able to:

text:
• Explain AI-Supported Public Health Workflows in plain language.
• Identify appropriate public health uses and limits.
• Describe human review, governance, equity, privacy, and monitoring expectations.
• Connect the topic to the related plays and tools.

text:
Use this slide in the AI-Supported Public Health Workflows training module.
Walk through the objectives and connect each one to a practical implementation decision.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 7, 10, 11 and Tools 31, 29, 41.
Explain that participants do not need technical expertise to use this material. The goal is enough shared understanding to participate in governance, readiness assessment, use case review, procurement, deployment, or monitoring.

AI is most useful when it is tied to a specific public health workflow rather than introduced as a general productivity tool. Workflow mapping helps teams identify where AI could support signal detection, evidence gathering, analysis, synthesis, communication, decision support, documentation, monitoring, or service delivery without displacing accountability.

Ask the group to restate the topic in their own words.

What event starts the workflow, and what output ends it? Where do staff spend time searching, summarizing, routing, checking, rewriting, or reconciling information? Which steps require judgment, legal authority, community relationship,.

text: Detect unusual patterns and prepare alerts for human review. Summarize reports, partner updates, inspection notes, or case records into structured fields. Draft situation reports, FAQs, or internal updates from approved source material. Track.
textPreview: Detect unusual patterns and prepare alerts for human review. Summarize reports, partner updates, inspection notes, or case records into s...

text: Keep AI support close to the staff workflow and test whether it actually saves time or improves quality. Measure false positives, false negatives, staff burden, equity effects, and downstream operational impact. Make handoffs explicit so staff.

text:
Use this slide in the AI-Supported Public Health Workflows training module.
Use this slide to turn the concept into practical behaviors, decisions, and safeguards.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 7, 10, 11 and Tools 31, 29, 41.
Invite participants to identify where these practices would need to be documented in their own department.
Start with Workflow Mapping Questions, then compare it with the other practice areas on the slide.
Use the topic to examine real workflows, not abstract technology.
textPreview: Use the topic to examine real workflows, not abstract technology.

text: Flag unusual changes in surveillance, laboratory, syndromic, environmental, or service-utilization data for staff investigation.
textPreview: Flag unusual changes in surveillance, laboratory, syndromic, environmental, or service-utilization data for staff investigation.

text: Combine situation reports, surveillance data, partner updates, and public communications into a concise operational update.
textPreview: Combine situation reports, surveillance data, partner updates, and public communications into a concise operational update.

text: Create an initial SitRep structure with current status, risks, actions taken, resource needs, and next operational decisions.
textPreview: Create an initial SitRep structure with current status, risks, actions taken, resource needs, and next operational decisions.

text:
Use this slide in the AI-Supported Public Health Workflows training module.
Use this slide to make the topic concrete through public health workflow examples.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 7, 10, 11 and Tools 31, 29, 41.
For each example, ask: What problem is being solved? What data would be used? Who reviews the AI output? What decision remains human? What could go wrong?

What problem in the workflow is AI expected to solve? Where does the workflow require professional judgment, legal authority, or community knowledge? How will staff know whether AI improves timeliness, quality, consistency, equity, or workload?

text: Choose one workflow and map the trigger, inputs, staff roles, decisions, outputs, handoffs, risks, and performance measures. Identify one AI support point and one step that should remain fully human-controlled.

Give participants time to work individually or in small groups. Ask them to produce a practical artifact that can feed into readiness assessment, governance review, use case prioritization, or implementation planning.
Expected takeaways include: AI workflow map, Workflow risk and control table, Pilot success measures.
Use this topic with Plays 7, 10, 11 and Tools 31, 29, 41. Turn the discussion into a documented question, decision, gap, or implementation artifact.
